title: Engineering Ligand and Receptor Pairs with LIPSTIC to Track Cell-Cell Interactions
author: Alberti, Guarniero, et al.
materials: pMP71 vector encoding GFP-P2A or Tomato-P2A coding sequences

steps.title: PCR amplification and Gibson assembly
steps.instruction: Add the following sequences to the coding sequences of LIPSTIC-engineered molecules:At the 5âĠ end: TGGAGGAGAACCCTGGACCGAt the 3âĠ end: AATTCGAGCATCTTACCGCC.This will ensure 20-bp overlap with the vector sequence. These sequences are identical for both receiving vectors pMP71-GFP-P2A and pMP71-Tomato-P2A.

steps.title: PCR amplification and Gibson assembly
steps.instruction: Order synthetic genes of the LIPSTIC-engineered molecules including the 20-bp overlap sequences specified in step 1.We routinely order synthetic genes from Integrated DNA Technology, but there are several synthetic gene providers available around the world.

steps.title: PCR amplification and Gibson assembly
steps.instruction: Amplify pMP71 vector using primers F1 and R1.Vectors are available upon request to the corresponding author. The F1 primer anneals after the 3âĠ end of the insert insertion site, and the R1 primer anneals at the 3âĠ end of the P2A sequence.Set up the reaction in PCR tubes as follows:1 ng template DNA1.5 Îỳl of 10 mM dNTPS2 Îỳl of 10 ÎỳM primer F12 Îỳl of 10 ÎỳM primer R110 Îỳl of 5Ã buffer (provided with LongAmpÂ® Taq DNA polymerase)2 Îỳl LongAmpÂ® Taq DNA polymeraseNuclease-free water to 50 Îỳl.Run PCR in a thermal cycler using the following cycle conditions: Initial denaturation94ÂḞC30 s30 cycles94ÂḞC30 s55ÂḞC60 s65ÂḞC5:50 minFinal extension65ÂḞC10 minHold4ÂḞC to 10ÂḞCâ

steps.title: PCR amplification and Gibson assembly
steps.instruction: Run PCR product on a 0.8% TAE agarose gel. Cut band and purify DNA using GeneJET Gel Extraction Kit according to the manufacturerâs instructions.The expected amplicon size is 6262 bp for pMP71-Tomato-P2A vector and 6335 bp for pMP71-GFP-P2A vector.

steps.title: PCR amplification and Gibson assembly
steps.instruction: Quantify concentration of the purified DNA.

steps.title: PCR amplification and Gibson assembly
steps.instruction: Perform Gibson assembly.Set up the reaction as follows:100 ng vector DNA (from step 4)3-fold molar excess (vs vector) insert DNA (from step 2)10 Îỳl Gibson assembly master mixNuclease-free water to 20 Îỳl.Incubate reaction at 50ÂḞC for 15 min, and then store on ice or at â20ÂḞC until step 7.We recommend cloning G5- and SrtA-tagged molecules in receiving vectors expressing different fluorescent reporters to easily distinguish differently functionalized cells in coculture experiments.

steps.title: Comparison with other methods
steps.instruction: Cell-cell interactions have long been investigated exclusively by imaging approaches. Thanks to these approaches, it is possible to visualize cell-cell contacts and to characterize multiple dynamic aspects of cell-cell interactions such as duration and frequency. When performed in vivo, live-imaging approaches can also reveal the microanatomical localization where interactions take place. Nevertheless, in this experimental setup, cells undergoing interactions cannot be identified and retrieved for downstream analysis, and therefore the phenotypic and molecular changes associated with the interaction cannot be investigated.

steps.title: Comparison with other methods
steps.instruction: In contrast with imaging, LIPSTIC offers the major advantage of marking the cells undergoing interaction in vitro and in vivo in a way that they can be later identified and isolated for downstream applications such as RNA sequencing and functional assays. Moreover, if a fluorescently labeled SrtA substrate is employed, LIPSTIC labeling can also be combined with live-imaging approaches to benefit from the advantages of both techniques in a single experiment.

steps.title: Comparison with other methods
steps.instruction: An emerging approach to monitoring cell-cell interactions is histocytometry, a high-content imaging approach that combines positional information with in-depth phenotypic characterization of cells by staining with multiple fluorescent probes (Gerner, Kasten-muller, Ifrim, Kabat, & Germain, 2012). Given its high resolution, this approach is also able to infer cell-cell communication based on proximity patterns, thus providing an anatomical description of the environment within which cell-cell interactions occur. We anticipate that this approach could be combined with the detection of LIPSTIC labeling to obtain a phenotypic characterization of interacting cells in situ. Collectively, the combination these different experimental techniques will provide unprecedented resolution to studies of the dynamics of the immune response and, in general, of cell-cell interactions.
